does: This paper proposes enhanced black-box jailbreak attacks against multimodal large language models that exploit both textual and visual modalities. The authors design provocative text prompts combined with visual prompts incorporating mutation and multi-image capabilities, and introduce a re-attack strategy to strengthen the evaluation. They empirically demonstrate that these methods effectively compromise both open-source and closed-source MLLMs. Beyond attacking, the paper identifies vulnerabilities in existing defenses and proposes new defense strategies operating at both training time and inference time, then evaluates these defensive measures against their jailbreak attacks to assess their effectiveness in mitigating the identified threats.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks and jailbreak methods that have targeted large language models, adapting them to the multimodal setting where both vision and language components must be considered. It builds on earlier findings showing that language models can be manipulated through carefully crafted prompts, while addressing the unique security challenges introduced by vision-language integration. The paper responds to growing concerns about MLLM safety gaps and complements existing literature on both attack mechanisms and defense strategies, leveraging knowledge from adversarial robustness, prompt engineering vulnerabilities, and multimodal learning to create more comprehensive security evaluations.
enables: This work establishes methodologies for systematically evaluating MLLM security that could inform the development of more robust multimodal systems. The identified defense gaps and proposed strategies provide a foundation for future work on hardening MLLMs against sophisticated attacks. The paper enables broader security assessment practices for both researchers and practitioners developing or deploying multimodal systems in sensitive applications. By demonstrating vulnerabilities across multiple attack vectors, it points toward the need for integrated defenses that account for multimodal interactions, potentially inspiring research into certified robustness for vision-language systems and more effective training procedures that better align MLLM behavior across both modalities.
